title: Integrated photon-pair sources on periodically poled thin-film lithium tantalate
authors: Sakthi Sanjeev Mohanraj, Xiaodong Shi, Ran Yang, Lin Zhou, Di Zhu
abstract: Chip-integrated photon-pair sources based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) have emerged as a promising solution for scalable quantum light generation. Thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT) is a compelling $χ^{(2)}$ platform, combining strong nonlinearity with a high optical-damage threshold, weak photorefractive response, and ferroelectricity that enables quasi-phase matching. However, SPDC-based photon-pair generation on TFLT has not yet been demonstrated. Here, we combine high-quality periodic poling with low-loss nanophotonic waveguides to realize photon-pair sources on TFLT in both traveling-wave and resonant configurations. In periodically poled straight waveguides, we achieve broadband photon-pair generation with high efficiency ($2.1~\mathrm{GHz}~\mathrm{mW}^{-1}$) and coincidence-to-accidental ratio (up to $3.8\times10^{5}$). We further confirm high-purity single-photon operation via heralded second-order correlation ($g^{(2)}_\mathrm{H}(0) = 0.0018 \pm 0.0002$) and high-fidelity time-energy entanglement through Franson interference (visibility of $98.9 \pm 0.5\%$). In periodically poled racetrack resonators, we map out a broad quantum frequency comb spanning the telecom C- and L-bands. By isolating individual frequency-correlated pairs, we measure a high spectral brightness of $11~\mathrm{GHz}~\mathrm{mW}^{-1}~\mathrm{GHz}^{-1}$. These results are competitive with the state of the art across $χ^{(2)}$ integrated platforms, positioning TFLT as a strong contender for integrated quantum light sources, with applications in wavelength-multiplexed quantum communications and photonic quantum information processing.
summary: This paper reports the first demonstration of photon-pair generation via spontaneous parametric down-conversion on a thin-film lithium tantalate platform. By using periodically poled waveguides and resonators, the authors achieved high-purity single photons and high-fidelity entanglement. This establishes TFLT as a competitive material for integrated quantum photonic circuits and wavelength-multiplexed quantum communications.
main_problem: The lack of demonstrated spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) based photon-pair generation on the thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT) platform, which is a promising candidate for scalable quantum light generation.
main_result: The authors demonstrated the first SPDC photon-pair generation in TFLT using both straight waveguides and racetrack resonators, achieving high-purity single photons and high-fidelity time-energy entanglement.
method: The researchers used periodic poling for quasi-phase matching in TFLT, combined with electron-beam lithography and dry etching. Characterization involved SHG/SFG spectroscopy, joint spectral intensity reconstruction, and Franson-type interferometry.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of periodically poled thin-film lithium tantalates (PPLT) in both traveling-wave (straight waveguides) and resonant (racetrack resonator) configurations.
key_observables: Photon-pair generation rate (PGR), coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR), heralded second-order correlation g2_H(0), Franson interference visibility, and joint spectral intensity (JSI).
important_parameters: Pair generation efficiency of 2.1 GHz mW^-1, CAR up to 3.8e5, g2_H(0) of 0.0018, and Franson visibility of 98.9%.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Fabrication process, device micrographs, and classical SHG/SFG characterization. Fig 2: Quantum characterization of waveguides including PGR and g2_H(tau). Fig 3: SHG power dependence. Fig 4: Quantum characterization of racetrack resonators.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the TFLT platform, details the fabrication and poling process, presents classical characterization of nonlinearities, demonstrates quantum photon-pair generation in waveguides, extends the demonstration to microresonators for frequency combs, and concludes with the platform's potential for quantum communications.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information because it establishes a new, high-performance integrated platform for generating entangled photon pairs and quantum frequency combs, essential for scalable quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Classical characterization of periodically poled lithium tantalate (PPLT) nanophotonic waveguide. a, (i) Schematic illustration of the electrical poling process on thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT). AWG: arbitrary waveform generator, HV amp: high-voltage amplifier, OSC: oscilloscope. b, (i) Optical micrograph of the PPLT nanophotonic waveguide, (ii) second-harmonic generation (SHG) confocal micrograph of the inverted domains after waveguide fabrication, and (iii) scanning electron micrograph (SEM) of the waveguide cross-section. c, On-chip SHG power as a function of pump power with normalized conversion efficiency of 841 ± 15 %W−1cm−2 in a 3-mm-long PPLT waveguide. Insets: measured...

FIG. 2. Nonclassical characterization of PPLT nanophotonic waveguide. a, Measurement setup for photon-pair generation. Vis laser: visible laser, SPF: short-pass filter, VOA: variable optical attenuator, OPM: optical power meter, PC: polarization controller, DUT: device under test, TEC: thermoelectric cooler, WDM: wavelength division multiplexer, LPF: long-pass filter, SNSPD: superconducting nanowire single-photon detector. b, Joint spectral intensity of the photon pairs with insets of the detection scheme (top) and SPDC spectrum obtained by optical spectrum analyzer (bottom). c, On-chip photon-pair generation rate (PGR) and coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR) as a function of pump power,...

FIG. 3. Classical characterization of PPLT racetrack resonator. a, Optical micrograph of PPLT racetrack resonator. b, SEM images of (i) waveguide cross-section and (ii) ring-bus waveguide coupling region, and (iii) SHG confocal microscopy image of the periodically poled region. c, Normalized transmission spectra of the SH and FH modes along with their respective quality factors. d, On-chip SHG power as a function of pump power with the normalized conversion efficiency of (3.9 ± 0.3) × 104 %W−1. Insets: PPLT racetrack transmission around FH wavelength showing 50 GHz free spectral range (FSR) and normalized SHG spectrum showing the optimal phase-matching wavelength.

FIG. 4. Non-classical characterization of PPLT racetrack resonator. a, (i) Schematic illustration of cavity enhanced SPDC process in TFLT racetrack resonator. (ii) The pump at wavelength of λp gives rise to comb-like discrete photon pairs centered near 2λp. (iii) Zoomed-in section of a measured quantum frequency comb indicating the 50 GHz comb line spacing and the signal-idler pair chosen for the measurements. b, Measured quantum frequency comb in the PPLT racetrack. c, Joint spectral intensity of the photon pairs, reconstructed from correlations across 30 DWDM channels with 50 GHz frequency spacing, displaying strong frequency correlation. d, On-chip PGR (red) with pair generation...
